rationale: The cited paper appears in the related-literature section as one example in a broad list of channel-coordination and cooperation studies. The citing paper summarizes it briefly as showing that bargaining can affect channel coordination when products are not completely specifiable, but it does not build its model, assumptions, or results on that bargaining framework. The main analysis in the citing paper uses its own sequential service-pricing model rather than extending or applying Iyer and Villas-Boas (2003).
